description: The Russian mafia is a collective of organized crime groups originating and operating in Russia, emerging prominently after the collapse of the Soviet Union in the 1990s. It is involved in a wide range of criminal activities including drug trafficking, human trafficking, and money laundering. The organization is highly structured and operates globally, with a quasi-military structure. Its relationship with the Russian state is pragmatic, with the state occasionally leveraging criminal networks for geopolitical purposes.

country_links.evidence_quote: Territory: Post-Soviet states , European Union , Israel, United States, Australia
country_links.context: The Russian mafia has territory in Australia.

country_links.evidence_quote: The Colombia -based Cali KGB Cartel supplied cocaine to the Ismailovskaya mafia, too.
country_links.context: The Colombia-based Cali KGB Cartel supplied cocaine to the Ismailovskaya mafia.

country_links.evidence_quote: establishing strongholds in Western European countries such as United Kingdom, Spain, Italy, Germany and France, as well as in Hungary and Israel and Czech Republic.
country_links.context: The Russian mafia established strongholds in the Czech Republic.

country_links.evidence_quote: In September 2025, a record cocaine seizure in Russia has highlighted the country’s growing role as a key destination for drug shipments originating from Ecuador
country_links.context: The Russian mafia sources cocaine from Ecuador, as evidenced by a record seizure of cocaine from Ecuador in Russia.

country_links.evidence_quote: Komarovskaya OPG ... organize the supply of cocaine from South America into Russia, Finland, Scandinavia and Europe and the trade in counterfeit dollars.
country_links.context: The Komarovskaya OPG organizes the supply of cocaine from South America into Finland.

country_links.evidence_quote: establishing strongholds in Western European countries such as United Kingdom, Spain, Italy, Germany and France, as well as in Hungary and Israel and Czech Republic.
country_links.context: The Russian mafia established strongholds in Israel.

country_links.evidence_quote: In 1995, the Camorra cooperated with the Russian mafia in a scheme in which the Camorra would bleach out US$1 bills and reprint them as $100s. These bills would then be transported to the Russian mafia for distribution in 29 post-Eastern Bloc countries and former Soviet republics. In return, the Russian mafia paid the Camorra with property (including a Russian bank) and firearms, smuggled into Eastern Europe and Italy.
country_links.context: The Russian mafia cooperated with the Camorra in Italy, laundering money and smuggling firearms into Italy.

country_links.evidence_quote: Russian organized crime was reported to have a strong grip in the French Riviera region and Spain in 2010; ... On 26 September 2017, as part of a 4-year investigation, 100 Spanish Civil Guard officers carried out 18 searches in different areas of Málaga, Spain, related to Russian mafia large scale money laundering.
country_links.context: The Russian mafia was reported to have a strong grip in Spain, and members were arrested for money laundering there.

country_links.evidence_quote: Mikhailov would later be arrested in Switzerland in October 1996 on numerous charges, including that he was the head of a powerful Russian mafia group
country_links.context: A Russian mafia boss was arrested in Switzerland on charges of heading a powerful group.

country_links.evidence_quote: Lasha Shushanashvili (born 1961): thief in law, who had significant influence in the criminal world of Moscow, Moscow Oblast, Chuvashia, Kirov Oblast, Ukraine and Spain.
country_links.context: Lasha Shushanashvili had significant influence in the criminal world of Ukraine.

country_links.evidence_quote: establishing strongholds in Western European countries such as United Kingdom, Spain, Italy, Germany and France, as well as in Hungary and Israel and Czech Republic.
country_links.context: The Russian mafia established strongholds in the United Kingdom.

country_links.evidence_quote: In 1992, it was agreed that Vyacheslav 'Yaponchik' Ivankov would be sent to Brighton Beach, U.S., allegedly because he was killing too many people in Russia and also to take control of Russian organized crime in North America. Within a year, he built an international operation that included but was not limited to narcotics, money laundering, and prostitution and made ties with the American Mafia and Colombian drug cartels, eventually extending to Miami, Los Angeles, and Boston.
country_links.context: The Russian mafia operates in the United States, with members based in cities like Brighton Beach, Miami, Los Angeles, and Boston, engaging in narcotics, money laundering, and prostitution.

country_links.evidence_quote: Vanuatu was a preferred location for Russian mafia laundering money.
country_links.context: Vanuatu was a preferred location for Russian mafia money laundering.
